content:
First, observe that on the 3×3 grid the adjacency‐graph is bipartite with 5 “black’’ squares and 4 “white’’ squares (e.g. in a checkerboard colouring the 4 corners plus the centre are black, the 4 edge‐centres are white).  Any two adjacent cells have opposite colours.

We must place the 5 odd numbers \{1,3,5,7,9\} and the 4 even numbers \{2,4,6,8\} so that adjacent cells have opposite parity.  Hence the odds must occupy all 5 black squares and the evens all 4 white squares.  Within each colour‐class one may permute freely, giving

    Total labelings = 5!·4! = 120·24 = 2880.

We now mod out by the four rotations (the group C₄).  By Burnside’s Lemma the number of orbits is

    (1/4)·( #fixed by id  +  #fixed by 90°  +  #fixed by 180°  +  #fixed by 270° ).

Clearly the identity fixes all 2880.  A 90° or 270° rotation acts on the 5 black squares as one 4‐cycle (the 4 corners) plus a 1‐cycle (the centre), and on the 4 white squares as a 4‐cycle (the edge‐centres).  To be fixed by such a rotation every cell in a nontrivial cycle would have to carry the same label—impossible with distinct labels.  Likewise a 180° rotation has two‐cycles among the corners and among the edges, so it too fixes no admissible labeling.  Hence

    #fixed by 90° = #fixed by 180° = #fixed by 270° = 0,

and the number of distinct arrangements up to rotation is

    (1/4)·(2880 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 720.

Answer: \boxed{720}.
